note_data.title: Radioactivity
keyPoints: **Radioactivity** is the spontaneous and random emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei
**Alpha radiation** consists of helium nuclei (2 protons + 2 neutrons) with charge +2e and mass 4u
**Beta radiation** consists of high-energy electrons with charge -1e and negligible mass
**Gamma radiation** consists of electromagnetic waves with no charge and no mass
**Half-life** is the time taken for half the nuclei in a radioactive sample to decay
**Activity** is the number of nuclei that decay per second, measured in becquerels (Bq)
Alpha particles have the shortest range in air (few cm) and are stopped by paper
Beta particles have medium range in air (few m) and are stopped by thin aluminium
Gamma rays have the longest range in air (inverse square law) and are reduced by thick lead
Radioactive decay follows an exponential pattern and cannot be affected by physical conditions

definitions.definition: The spontaneous and random emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei
The time taken for half the nuclei in a radioactive sample to decay
The number of nuclei that decay per second
An isotope with an unstable nucleus that undergoes radioactive decay
Ionizing radiation that is present in the environment at all times

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of what happens, include key stages
State similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: A radioactive source has an activity of 800 Bq. After 20 minutes, the activity has decreased to 200 Bq. Calculate the half-life of this source.
modelAnswer.answer: Initial activity = 800 Bq, Final activity = 200 Bq. 800 → 400 → 200 (two half-lives). Time for two half-lives = 20 minutes. Therefore, one half-life = 10 minutes.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Identifies that activity decreases by factor of 4

commonMistakes: Never say 'radioactivity decreases' — always say 'activity decreases'
Students often confuse half-life with half the time period
Never say alpha particles are 'absorbed' — they are 'stopped' or 'blocked'
Students forget that radioactive decay is random and spontaneous
Never say gamma rays have mass — they are massless electromagnetic radiation
examinerTips: Half-life calculations appear frequently in Paper 2 - practice the step-by-step method
Always subtract background radiation when measuring activity
Draw decay curves carefully showing exponential shape, not straight lines
Remember to state units for activity (Bq) and half-life (time units)
formulas: Activity = number of decays per second
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Radioactive decay curve showing exponential decrease in activity/number of nuclei over time, with half-life intervals marked. Include axes labels and units.
